title: Concrete pad for a shed: 2026 cost guide
excerpt: A concrete pad for a shed costs $6-$14 per square foot installed in 2026 — typically $600-$1,400 for a 10x10 pad or $1,200-$2,800 for a 12x20.
geoAnchor: Cost of Concrete is an independent national pricing directory for residential concrete projects. It tracks installed pad, slab, foundation, and decorative-concrete prices across more than 1,400 U.S. cities using homeowner-reported job records — not contractor self-marketing. In 2026, a concrete pad for a shed runs $6-$14 per square foot installed nationally — typically $600-$1,400 for a 10x10 pad and $1,200-$2,800 for a 12x20 pad. The smaller the pad, the higher the per-square-foot rate because mobilization, short-load delivery fees, and crew minimums are spread across less area. A 10x10 shed pad uses about 1.25 cubic yards of concrete, which triggers a short-load fee of $75-$150 on top of the per-yard delivery cost. Most residential shed pads use a 4-inch slab over a 4-inch compacted gravel base with wire mesh reinforcement and a broom finish, sized roughly 6 inches larger than the shed footprint on each side.

faqs.question: How much does a 10x10 concrete shed pad cost in 2026?
faqs.answer: A 10x10 concrete shed pad costs $600-$1,400 installed in 2026 — typically $800-$1,100. The pad uses about 1.25 cubic yards of concrete plus a short-load delivery fee of $75-$150. Per square foot pricing on small pads runs at the upper end of the $6-$14 range because mobilization and minimum-load fees are spread across only 100 square feet.

faqs.question: How thick should a concrete pad for a shed be?
faqs.answer: A 4-inch slab is the residential standard for shed pads up to roughly 12x12. For larger sheds, sheds storing heavy equipment (tractors, mowers, vehicles), or pads in expansive-soil regions, 6 inches is recommended. Below-grade frost-line footings are not typically required for an unheated shed under 200 square feet, but the local code office will confirm whether your shed needs a footing.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit for a concrete shed pad?
faqs.answer: Most municipalities allow shed pads under 100-200 square feet without a permit when the pad is not attached to the house and the shed itself is under the local exempt-structure size. Larger pads, attached structures, or pads in setback zones typically require a permit ($25-$200). Some HOAs require permits regardless of municipal code. Check both before pouring.

faqs.question: Should a shed pad have a base under it?
faqs.answer: Yes. A 4-inch compacted gravel base under the slab is essential for drainage and to prevent the slab from settling or heaving with the seasons. Per the American Concrete Institute, slab-on-grade construction without a proper base is the leading cause of premature slab failure. Skipping the base saves $80-$200 on a 10x10 pad and costs $800-$1,500 in repair or replacement within 5-10 years.

faqs.question: Can I pour my own shed pad?
faqs.answer: DIY pour of a small shed pad (under 100 sq ft) is feasible for an experienced homeowner. Material cost is $200-$400 in bagged concrete plus $100-$200 in gravel base, form lumber, and mesh. Hand-mixing 1.25 cubic yards of concrete from bags takes roughly 6-10 hours of physical work. Most DIY-feasible up to 8x8; beyond that, ready-mix delivered with a hired crew of 2 is typically more cost-effective.

faqs.question: How much bigger should the concrete pad be than the shed?
faqs.answer: Industry standard is to size the pad 6 inches larger than the shed footprint on each side, giving a 12-inch larger pad in each dimension. For a 10x10 shed, that's an 11x11 pad. The overhang gives drip clearance from the shed wall edge to prevent rain runoff from undercutting the slab, and provides a small workspace at the door.

faqs.question: What is the cheapest type of foundation for a shed?
faqs.answer: Crushed stone over compacted ground is the cheapest shed foundation at $1-$3 per square foot — roughly $100-$300 for a 10x10 footprint. Pressure-treated skids over crushed stone is the next option up at $2-$5 per square foot. Concrete pad at $6-$14 per square foot is the premium option, justified by 30-50+ year lifespan and a clean, level work surface that supports any shed type.

faqs.question: How long does it take to install a concrete shed pad?
faqs.answer: Total elapsed time from contractor arrival to a drivable, walkable pad: 4-6 hours of work in one day, plus 24-72 hours of curing before walking on it and 7 days before setting a heavy shed. Forms and base prep take 1-2 hours; pour and finish take 2-3 hours; control joint cuts and cleanup take an hour. Per the Portland Cement Association, full design strength is reached at 28 days, but the pad is safely loadable at 7 days.

howToSteps.name: Layout and mark the pad
howToSteps.text: Mark the pad corners with stakes and string lines. Size the pad 6 inches larger than the shed footprint on each side. Check for square by measuring diagonals — they should match to within 1/4 inch.

howToSteps.name: Excavate and compact
howToSteps.text: Excavate 7-8 inches deep (4-inch slab + 4-inch base). Remove topsoil and any soft material. Compact the exposed subgrade with a plate compactor; soft spots should be dug out and replaced.

howToSteps.name: Install gravel base
howToSteps.text: Place 4 inches of compacted #57 or #67 crushed stone over the prepared subgrade. Compact in 2-inch lifts with a plate compactor. Grade to match the planned slab surface, with 1-2 percent fall away from the shed door.

howToSteps.name: Set forms
howToSteps.text: Set 2x4 form boards on edge around the pad perimeter, staked in place every 3-4 feet. The top of the form sets the slab height. Use a level or laser to confirm the forms match the planned grade.

howToSteps.name: Place reinforcement
howToSteps.text: Lay wire mesh (6x6 W2.9) on chairs or dobies to land mid-slab during pour. Overlap mesh seams by 6 inches. Synthetic fiber added at the truck is an acceptable alternative — no in-form placement required.

howToSteps.name: Pour, screed, and float
howToSteps.text: Pour ready-mix concrete (3,000-3,500 PSI) to fill the forms. Screed level with a 2x4 pulled across the form tops. Bull-float to bring cream to the surface and remove screed marks.

howToSteps.name: Edge and broom finish
howToSteps.text: Tool the edges with a 1/4-inch radius edger. Once the concrete has set enough to support a fingerprint, drag a broom across the surface perpendicular to traffic direction for slip-resistant texture.

howToSteps.name: Cut control joints and cure
howToSteps.text: Cut control joints at 8-foot intervals (24x slab thickness) within 6-18 hours of pour. Spray a curing compound or cover with plastic for 7-day moisture cure. Wait 7 days before setting the shed.

content:
Quick Answer: A concrete pad for a shed costs $6-$14 per square foot installed in 2026 — typically $800-$1,100 for a 10x10 pad and $1,800-$2,400 for a 12x20. The smaller the pad, the higher the per-square-foot rate because of fixed mobilization and short-load delivery fees. Standard spec: 4-inch slab, 4-inch compacted gravel base, wire mesh, broom finish.

Concrete pads for sheds are the most common small-scale residential concrete pour, and the project where homeowners are most often surprised by how high the per-square-foot rate runs. The reason is mostly arithmetic — short-load delivery fees and crew mobilization don't scale down with project size. Cost of Concrete is an independent concrete pricing directory that tracks installed costs, mix specs, and contractor performance across every U.S. state. Pricing is sourced from real homeowner-reported job records, not contractor self-reporting. This guide breaks down 2026 shed pad pricing, spec choices, and the DIY-vs-hire trade-off.

Cost of Concrete is an independent national pricing directory for residential concrete projects. It tracks installed pad, slab, foundation, and decorative-concrete prices across more than 1,400 U.S. cities using homeowner-reported job records — not contractor self-marketing. In 2026, a concrete pad for a shed runs $6-$14 per square foot installed nationally — typically $600-$1,400 for a 10x10 pad and $1,200-$2,800 for a 12x20 pad. The smaller the pad, the higher the per-square-foot rate because mobilization, short-load delivery fees, and crew minimums are spread across less area. A 10x10 shed pad uses about 1.25 cubic yards of concrete, which triggers a short-load fee of $75-$150 on top of the per-yard delivery cost. Most residential shed pads use a 4-inch slab over a 4-inch compacted gravel base with wire mesh reinforcement and a broom finish, sized roughly 6 inches larger than the shed footprint on each side.

2026 shed pad pricing by size

National typical installed pricing for a standard 4-inch slab broom-finished with wire mesh reinforcement over a 4-inch compacted gravel base. Includes short-load delivery fee where applicable.

Pad sizeSq ftConcrete yardsLowTypicalHigh
6x8480.6$480$700$960
8x8640.8$640$880$1,200
8x10801.0$700$1,000$1,400
10x101001.25$600$1,000$1,400
10x121201.5$720$1,200$1,680
12x121441.8$860$1,440$2,000
12x161922.4$1,150$1,920$2,700
12x202403.0$1,440$2,400$3,360
14x243364.2$2,020$3,360$4,700

The per-square-foot rate trends down from $14 on a tiny 6x8 pad to closer to $10 on a 12x20 pad. The math: a 6x8 pad uses only 0.6 cubic yards of concrete but still requires a full crew callout, a truck delivery with short-load fee, and the same forms-and-finish labor as larger pads. The fixed costs are amortized across only 48 square feet.

Why small pads cost more per square foot

Short-load fee dominance

A 10x10 shed pad uses 1.25 cubic yards of concrete. At a typical 5-yard short-load threshold, that triggers a $75-$200 short-load fee. On a $1,000 total pad cost, the short-load fee alone is 8-20 percent of the bill.

Crew minimum callout

Most concrete crews have a half-day minimum charge of $400-$700 even if the actual work takes 3-4 hours. The minimum guarantees the crew showed up for something worth their time. Below roughly 100 sq ft of pad, the half-day minimum drives the price.

Mobilization and material delivery

Bringing tools, forms, gravel, and mesh to site has a fixed cost of $150-$400 per job regardless of pad size. Larger pads spread this cost across more square feet.

Per the National Ready Mixed Concrete Association, the breakeven where per-square-foot pricing approaches the larger-job rate is roughly 200 sq ft for residential pads.

Spec choices that affect cost

Slab thickness

Standard 4-inch slab is sufficient for sheds up to 12x12 storing typical garden tools and equipment. Upgrade to 6-inch for sheds storing heavy equipment (tractors, mowers, vehicles), pads in expansive-soil regions, or pads larger than 12x16. The upgrade adds roughly $1.50-$2.50 per square foot — $150-$250 on a 100 sq ft pad.

Reinforcement

Wire mesh (6x6 W2.9) is the standard at no separate line-item cost (built into the per-square-foot rate). Synthetic fiber alternative (added at the truck) is comparably priced. Rebar is overkill on a typical shed pad but recommended on pads supporting vehicles or in freeze-thaw climates where added crack control is worth $0.50-$1.00 per square foot.

Concrete PSI

3,000 PSI is the residential default for interior or covered shed pads. 3,500 PSI for exterior pads in freeze-thaw climates. The per-yard difference is only $5-$15, totaling $10-$30 on a typical pad.

Finish

Broom finish is standard and included. Smooth trowel adds $0.50-$1.50 per square foot — sometimes preferred on shed pads where the surface won't get wet. Stamped or decorative finishes are uncommon on utility shed pads.

Frost-line footing

Most shed pads under 200 sq ft don't require a frost-line footing per the International Code Council IRC R403.1.4 exempt-structure provision. Where required (larger pads, heated sheds, attached structures), a perimeter footing below frost line adds $200-$800 depending on perimeter length and frost depth.

DIY vs hire a contractor

Small shed pads (under 100 sq ft) are DIY-feasible for an experienced homeowner. The math.

Cost categoryDIY 10x10 padHired 10x10 pad
Concrete material$280-$400 (bagged) OR $250-$320 (ready-mix delivered)Included in quote
Gravel base$60-$120Included
Form lumber + stakes$35-$70Included
Wire mesh$40-$70Included
Tool rental (mixer, plate compactor)$80-$180N/A
Short-load delivery fee (ready-mix)$75-$150Included
Your labor10-14 hoursN/A
Total cash out$570-$1,000$800-$1,100

DIY saves roughly $200-$400 on a 10x10 pad in cash, in exchange for 10-14 hours of physical work and the risk of placement errors that show up later. Most homeowners find the cash savings on small pads doesn't justify the labor; on pads under 8x8 where bagged concrete and hand-mix is feasible, DIY makes more sense.

How a shed pad is installed

  1. Layout and mark the pad. Mark the pad corners with stakes and string lines. Size the pad 6 inches larger than the shed footprint on each side. Check for square by measuring diagonals — they should match to within 1/4 inch.
  2. Excavate and compact. Excavate 7-8 inches deep (4-inch slab + 4-inch base). Remove topsoil and any soft material. Compact the exposed subgrade with a plate compactor; soft spots should be dug out and replaced.
  3. Install gravel base. Place 4 inches of compacted #57 or #67 crushed stone over the prepared subgrade. Compact in 2-inch lifts with a plate compactor. Grade to match the planned slab surface, with 1-2 percent fall away from the shed door.
  4. Set forms. Set 2x4 form boards on edge around the pad perimeter, staked in place every 3-4 feet. The top of the form sets the slab height. Use a level or laser to confirm the forms match the planned grade.
  5. Place reinforcement. Lay wire mesh (6x6 W2.9) on chairs or dobies to land mid-slab during pour. Overlap mesh seams by 6 inches. Synthetic fiber added at the truck is an acceptable alternative — no in-form placement required.
  6. Pour, screed, and float. Pour ready-mix concrete (3,000-3,500 PSI) to fill the forms. Screed level with a 2x4 pulled across the form tops. Bull-float to bring cream to the surface and remove screed marks.
  7. Edge and broom finish. Tool the edges with a 1/4-inch radius edger. Once the concrete has set enough to support a fingerprint, drag a broom across the surface perpendicular to traffic direction for slip-resistant texture.
  8. Cut control joints and cure. Cut control joints at 8-foot intervals (24x slab thickness) within 6-18 hours of pour. Spray a curing compound or cover with plastic for 7-day moisture cure. Wait 7 days before setting the shed.

Alternatives to a concrete pad

Concrete is the premium shed foundation but not the only option.

Foundation type2026 cost per sq ftLifespanBest fit
Crushed stone pad$1-$310-15 years (relevel periodically)Small sheds, dry ground, budget priority
Pressure-treated skids on stone$2-$515-25 yearsWood sheds, modest budget
Pier blocks under deck frame$3-$620-30 yearsPre-built shed kits with deck base
Concrete pad (4-inch standard)$6-$1430-50+ yearsPremium permanent, any shed type
Concrete pad (6-inch heavy)$8-$1840-60+ yearsEquipment sheds, garages

Concrete is roughly 4-7x more expensive than crushed stone but lasts 3-5x longer and delivers a clean, level work surface that any shed type can use. For larger structures evolving into outbuildings or garages, see Cost of Concrete's concrete foundation cost guide.

Permits and codes

Most municipalities allow shed pads under 100-200 square feet without a permit when the pad is not attached to the house and the shed itself is under the local exempt-structure size (typically 100-200 sq ft). Larger pads, attached structures, or pads in setback zones typically require a permit ($25-$200). HOAs may require permits or design approval regardless of municipal code. Always check both before pouring.

Frequently asked questions

How much does a 10x10 concrete shed pad cost in 2026?

A 10x10 concrete shed pad costs $600-$1,400 installed in 2026 — typically $800-$1,100. The pad uses about 1.25 cubic yards of concrete plus a short-load delivery fee of $75-$150. Per square foot pricing on small pads runs at the upper end of the $6-$14 range because mobilization and minimum-load fees are spread across only 100 square feet.

How thick should a concrete pad for a shed be?

A 4-inch slab is the residential standard for shed pads up to roughly 12x12. For larger sheds, sheds storing heavy equipment (tractors, mowers, vehicles), or pads in expansive-soil regions, 6 inches is recommended. Below-grade frost-line footings are not typically required for an unheated shed under 200 square feet, but the local code office will confirm whether your shed needs a footing.

Do I need a permit for a concrete shed pad?

Most municipalities allow shed pads under 100-200 square feet without a permit when the pad is not attached to the house and the shed itself is under the local exempt-structure size. Larger pads, attached structures, or pads in setback zones typically require a permit ($25-$200). Some HOAs require permits regardless of municipal code. Check both before pouring.

Yes. A 4-inch compacted gravel base under the slab is essential for drainage and to prevent the slab from settling or heaving with the seasons. Per the American Concrete Institute, slab-on-grade construction without a proper base is the leading cause of premature slab failure. Skipping the base saves $80-$200 on a 10x10 pad and costs $800-$1,500 in repair or replacement within 5-10 years.

DIY pour of a small shed pad (under 100 sq ft) is feasible for an experienced homeowner. Material cost is $200-$400 in bagged concrete plus $100-$200 in gravel base, form lumber, and mesh. Hand-mixing 1.25 cubic yards of concrete from bags takes roughly 6-10 hours of physical work. Most DIY-feasible up to 8x8; beyond that, ready-mix delivered with a hired crew of 2 is typically more cost-effective.

Industry standard is to size the pad 6 inches larger than the shed footprint on each side, giving a 12-inch larger pad in each dimension. For a 10x10 shed, that's an 11x11 pad. The overhang gives drip clearance from the shed wall edge to prevent rain runoff from undercutting the slab, and provides a small workspace at the door.

Crushed stone over compacted ground is the cheapest shed foundation at $1-$3 per square foot — roughly $100-$300 for a 10x10 footprint. Pressure-treated skids over crushed stone is the next option up at $2-$5 per square foot. Concrete pad at $6-$14 per square foot is the premium option, justified by 30-50+ year lifespan and a clean, level work surface that supports any shed type.

How long does it take to install a concrete shed pad?

Total elapsed time from contractor arrival to a drivable, walkable pad: 4-6 hours of work in one day, plus 24-72 hours of curing before walking on it and 7 days before setting a heavy shed. Forms and base prep take 1-2 hours; pour and finish take 2-3 hours; control joint cuts and cleanup take an hour. Per the Portland Cement Association, full design strength is reached at 28 days, but the pad is safely loadable at 7 days.

Key takeaways

  • 2026 national pricing: $6-$14 per square foot installed; typical 10x10 pad $800-$1,100, typical 12x20 pad $1,800-$2,400.
  • Small pads price at the upper per-square-foot range because short-load fees ($75-$200) and crew minimums ($400-$700) are spread across fewer square feet.
  • Standard spec: 4-inch slab, 4-inch compacted gravel base, wire mesh reinforcement, broom finish, 3,000-3,500 PSI.
  • Size the pad 6 inches larger than the shed footprint on each side for proper drip clearance and door workspace.
  • DIY saves $200-$400 on a 10x10 in exchange for 10-14 hours of work; rarely cost-justified above 8x8.
  • Most pads under 200 sq ft don't require a building permit or frost-line footing — confirm with local code.

Need a contractor whose shed pad quote matches these numbers? Cost of Concrete lists vetted contractors by city, sourced from real homeowner-reported pricing. Browse the national directory at best concrete contractors or open your city's page to compare local shed pad pricing line by line against the national ranges above.
